title: Galois groups of random integer polynomials
speaker: Manjul Bharğava
abstract: In this three-part lecture series, we will present a series of works by Bedrossian, Blumenthal and Punshon-Smith on the chaotic mixing and enhanced dissipation properties of a passive tracer subject to the motion of an ergodic Markovian flow of spatially regular incompressible velocity fields on a compact domain. Such flows crucially include the motion of a velocity field solving the stochastic Navier-Stokes equations in T2 (or T3 with hyperviscosity), but can be more general or much simpler in character.

proof_stages.label: Van der Waerden conjecture and main theorem
proof_stages.summary: The talk formulates the count E_n(h) of monic integer degree-n polynomials of height at most h whose Galois group is not S_n, explains the sharp lower bound of order h^{n-1}, reviews previous quantitative Hilbert-irreducibility bounds, and states the goal E_n(h)=O(h^{n-1}), more generally via counts N_n(G,h) for permutation groups G≤S_n.

proof_stages.label: Reduction away from intransitive and imprimitive groups
proof_stages.summary: Known estimates for reducible polynomials handle intransitive Galois groups, and bounds obtained by counting fields with nontrivial subfields handle transitive imprimitive groups, so it remains to treat primitive permutation groups G<S_n.

proof_stages.label: Jordan transposition criterion and squarefull discriminants
proof_stages.summary: Jordan's theorem that a primitive subgroup of S_n containing a transposition is all of S_n implies that a primitive non-S_n Galois group has no transposition inertia, hence the associated number-field discriminant is squarefull at every ramified prime.

proof_stages.label: Three-way decomposition by ramified primes and discriminant
proof_stages.summary: For K_f=Q[x]/(f), with C the product of ramified primes and D=|Disc(K_f)|, the remaining polynomials are divided into the regimes C≤h with D≥h^2, D≤h^2, and C>h, according to which counting method can apply.

proof_stages.label: Large discriminant with moderate ramified-prime product
proof_stages.summary: When C≤h and D≥h^2, the necessary ramification conditions are congruence conditions modulo C of density essentially D^{-1}, so direct lattice-point counting in the coefficient box and summation over squarefull D gives O(h^{n-1+ε}).

proof_stages.label: Small discriminant via field counts and generator counts
proof_stages.summary: When D≤h^2, Schmidt-type bounds count the possible degree-n fields and a separate bound counts how many height-h monic polynomials generate a fixed field, with sharper known low-degree field counts filling the remaining small-degree cases.

proof_stages.label: Large ramified-prime product and the double discriminant
proof_stages.summary: When C>h, squarefull ramification forces the polynomial discriminant to vanish mod p^2 for mod-p reasons, which via a derivative criterion makes the double discriminant DD(a_1,...,a_{n-1}) divisible by C; after excluding DD=0 and fixing the first n−1 coefficients, this bounds the remaining coefficient and yields O(h^{n-1+ε}).

proof_stages.label: Removal of epsilon losses
proof_stages.summary: The ε-losses are removed by thickening the first two discriminant ranges, proving an equidistribution/Fourier estimate for the relevant congruence classes modulo C slightly larger than h, and in the large-C case replacing C by a suitable divisor C′ so that either the Fourier argument applies or C′ has boundedly many prime factors and the double-discriminant argument loses no h^ε.

examples.name: D4 acting on the vertices of a square
examples.note: In response to a question, the speaker uses the dihedral group D4, transitive on four vertices but preserving the partition into diagonals, to make the definition of an imprimitive transitive permutation group concrete.
examples.engagement_reason: he uses D4 on a square to exhibit imprimitive transitivity by identifying the diagonal partition {1,3},{2,4} and checking that the action preserves it

examples.name: Monogenicity of rings of integers
examples.note: In Q&A, the speaker points to the open problem of whether a given ring of integers is generated by one algebraic integer as an illustration of why polynomial-counting and field-counting statistics differ.
examples.engagement_reason: monogenicity is only raised as an open issue about whether a ring of integers is generated by one element, with no construction or calculation carried out

passes.reason: Monogenicity is invoked as an ordinary hard property of rings of integers relating fields to polynomial presentations, not as a special object or refutation.

examples.name: The Mathieu group M11 as a test case
examples.note: The speaker cites G=M11 as a customary benchmark for structure-sensitive bounds on N_n(G,h), illustrating that the method gives better exponents for particular primitive groups.
examples.engagement_reason: M11 is cited as the customary test case and some bounds are stated, but the method is not worked through on the group

examples.name: Sparse/even polynomial families with Weyl-group generic Galois group
examples.note: The family obtained by forcing alternating coefficients to vanish is mentioned as a related random-polynomial setting where the generic Galois group is a Weyl group and the same techniques give different exponents.
examples.engagement_reason: he discusses the sparse/even family concretely, identifies the Weyl-group generic cases and sketches how reducing to an associated polynomial with S_n and a square condition gives the stated counts

passes.reason: The sparse even families are systematic parity-symmetric polynomial families with Weyl-group generic Galois group, presented as variants rather than counterexamples.

examples.name: Cubic surfaces and the 27 lines with W(E6) action
examples.note: As a favorite further question, the speaker asks whether analogous large-sieve-type savings can be proved for the Galois action on the 27 lines of a cubic surface, whose generic group is W(E6).
examples.engagement_reason: cubic surfaces and their 27 lines with W(E6) action are presented as a natural open question, not analyzed further
